That motivation led me to train as a medical laboratory technologist, where examining blood samples and diagnostic tests showed me how early detection can alter the course of hematologic malignancies. Wanting to move from diagnosis to discovery, I pursued a master’s degree in hematology. During my PhD at Ludwig Maximilian University of Munich, I focused on understanding the epigenetic mechanisms that regulate hematopoietic development. As my training deepened, I became increasingly fascinated by the Nobel Prize-winning immunotherapy and its transformative potential. Leveraging my background in hematology and immunology, I shifted my focus toward optimizing immunotherapeutic strategies for patients with hematologic malignancies. Today, I remain guided by a clear sense of purpose—my “why.” As a cancer researcher, I aim to translate scientific insight into immunotherapeutic approaches that improve outcomes, extend survival, and offer patients lasting hope. Monitoring Longitudinal Immune Fitness Q | Tell us about your favorite research project and what you’re currently working on. One of my favorite research projects explored how T cell immune fitness changes over time in patients with acute myeloid leukemia (AML). This question arose from the observation that several bispecific T cell engager antibodies have not yielded sustained responses in patients with patients. By testing patient-derived T cells against bispecific T cell engager antibodies at different disease stages, I found that T cells exhibit distinct functional states at diagnosis and relapse compared to complete remission. Using an integrated phenotypic, functional, metabolic, transcriptomic, and epigenomic approach, this work provided a mechanistic rationale for moving bispecific antibodies to earlier treatment settings, such as consolidation therapy—particularly in patients in remission with minimal residual disease—where restored T cell function and favorable effector-to-target ratios provide a window of enhanced therapeutic responsiveness and improved clinical outcomes. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cancer updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Building on this work, my current research expands longitudinal immune monitoring to a broader population of patients with cancer on active treatments, including both hematologic malignancies and solid tumors. Using high-dimensional immunophenotyping and activation-induced marker assays, I aim to define T cell immune fitness as a biomarker that can help predict treatment responses and guide more personalized immunotherapy strategies. This large-scale study of approximately 5,000 patient samples is designed to serve as a shared resource for the cancer immunology community. Q | What has been a rewarding aspect of your career so far? The most exciting part of my scientific journey has been seeing how passion, patience, and perseverance have enabled me to conduct high-impact, clinically relevant translational research with the potential to benefit patients with cancer. It has been deeply rewarding to see my work published in leading journals such as Leukemia , Blood , and PNAS , and it was particularly meaningful that two of my favorite projects were recognized with the American Society of Hematology Abstract Achievement Award. These milestones reinforced my commitment to pursuing research that bridges scientific insights and patient care. Equally exciting have been the people I have encountered along the way. Working alongside highly driven scientists who excel professionally while maintaining fulfilling personal lives reshaped how I think about success. I learned that there is no single definition of work-life balance; instead, work-life integration evolves over time. Accepting moments of exhaustion while continuing to move forward with purpose has been an important lesson—one that continues to guide me as a scientist. Q | If you could be a laboratory instrument, which one would you be and why? I would be a fluorescence-activated cell sorter ( FACS ). A FACS machine has the unique ability to bring clarity by resolving heterogeneous cell populations into defined, functional subsets. In many ways, this mirrors my scientific approach—seeking to understand immune diversity rather than averaging it away. Just as a FACS machine identifies and isolates rare yet meaningful cell populations, my research focuses on uncovering critical immune states that drive treatment response or resistance in patients with cancer. Stratifying cells based on their functional and phenotypic characteristics is central to precision immunotherapy, where identifying the right immune context can determine clinical success. Beyond its technical power, FACS represents rigor, reproducibility, and the translation of complex data into actionable insights. These principles guide my work as I aim to shed light on immune mechanisms and apply them toward more effective and personalized cancer therapies. Responses have been edited for length and clarity. Innovation Spotlight Precision Immunology: Redefining How We Treat Immune-Mediated Disease A new era of translating clinical insights into durable, patient-centered care is on the horizon. Written by Kori Wallace, MD, PhD Kori Wallace, MD, PhD Kori Wallace serves as the Vice President of Clinical Development for AbbVie Immunology, where she creates and implements the strategic vision for the organization's portfolio of assets ranging from pre-IND View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Jan 31, 2026 | 4 min read Save for Later Scientific innovation is driving a shift from one-size-fits-all care to personalized strategies in immune disease treatment. Image credit: AbbVie Immunology PDF version Share Kori Wallace is the vice president of Clinical Development for AbbVie Immunology The promise of precision medicine in immunology is both daunting and profound: tailoring treatment to each person’s unique biology to achieve better outcomes. This approach has revolutionized oncology, demonstrating the success of matching treatments to molecular biomarkers. Immunology brings new challenges: immune‑mediated conditions are heterogeneous, influenced by multiple overlapping immune and inflammatory pathways, and can change over time. Yet even in the face of that complexity, new approaches grounded in reverse translation, rational combination strategies, and molecular profiling can pave a path toward higher remission rates and more predictable, patient‑centered care. Current therapies for many immune-mediated diseases often plateau at similar efficacy levels. Even when a new mechanism adds a meaningful bump, a substantial portion of people still do not achieve or maintain response at one year. This “efficacy ceiling” reflects multifactorial biology: suppressing one pathway can leave others active, allowing symptoms to persist or return. For patients, that reality translates into cycling through treatments, preventable flares, and uncertainty. A more precise strategy, built around a person’s molecular drivers, aims to reduce that uncertainty and deliver steadier relief with treatment that reflects the diversity and complexity of each individual’s immune system. At AbbVie, this ambition is shaping how we think about the future of immunology. Reverse Translation: Closing the Gap Between Research and Patient Care Reverse translation is increasingly reshaping immunology research by turning real-world evidence and knowledge generated from ongoing clinical trials of investigational medicines into discovery insights and then cycling those insights back into drug development. Instead of relying solely on preclinical hypotheses, researchers can now systematically analyze clinical data and patient samples to uncover new patterns and mechanisms that might otherwise remain hidden. This approach acknowledges the multifactorial and evolving nature of immune-mediated diseases, where a single pathway rarely tells the whole story. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Immunology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe However, the process is not without hurdles. Obtaining tissue samples, for example, can be invasive and logistically challenging, limiting the granularity of findings. In addition, the heterogeneity intrinsic to autoimmune diseases means that relevant signals are often subtle and complex, not easily captured by a single biomarker. As a result, reverse translation increasingly relies on advanced analytical approaches, using large datasets and sophisticated modeling techniques, to uncover patterns. Despite these challenges, the approach is paving the path toward more precise care in immunology, ensuring that discoveries are continually informed and refined by real-world outcomes. Rational Combination Strategies: Breaking the Efficacy Ceiling The insights gained from reverse translation are now fueling a new era of therapeutic innovation in immunology, the design of rational combination strategies that pair complementary mechanisms to overcome efficacy ceilings. The goal is not simply to “stack” drugs, but to identify pathway synergies that produce biological effects neither agent achieves alone. It is the lessons learned from nonresponders that are proving particularly valuable. By examining what continues to drive disease when symptoms persist, researchers can uncover which immune pathways remain active or insufficiently suppressed. That knowledge informs the design of rational combination approaches that maintain control of the original pathway while adding a second or even a third mechanism to address residual drivers of disease. In immunology, this concept is still maturing, but early data suggest that such combinations can sometimes uncover previously unrecognized biological interactions. Yet even the most promising combinations must ultimately prove their value where it matters most—for real patients in the clinic. The emergence of novel biology is only meaningful if it translates into tangible improvements in remission rates, durability of response, or quality of life. Achieving this requires not just new therapies, but also new ways of identifying which patients are most likely to benefit, which is a challenge that brings molecular profiling to the forefront of precision immunology. Molecular Profiling: Designing Studies That Impact the Biology Driving Disease Molecular profiling is shifting immunology beyond broad diagnostic categories toward a more nuanced, biology-driven approach. By analyzing molecular signatures—unique patterns of genes, proteins, and other biomarkers—researchers can stratify people into subgroups that are more likely to respond to specific therapies. This approach can make it possible to match the right drug, or combination of drugs, to the right person at the right time. In clinical trials, molecular profiling can enable researchers to test insights gained from reverse translation, focusing on whether a targeted pathway is mechanistically relevant to a specific subgroup. These mechanistic trials are key to validating such hypotheses, but they are challenging. Unlike oncology, where tumor shrinkage offers a clear signal, immunology endpoints often involve subjective measures, such as joint tenderness or abdominal pain, and variable biomarkers, making efficacy harder to detect in small cohorts. Regulatory frameworks add complexity, as current standards still favor large, all-comer studies. Adapting regulations to support mechanistic trials will be critical for accelerating innovation. When mechanistic trials confirm a pathway’s relevance, they feed directly into larger studies designed to establish safety and efficacy—steps required before new therapies can reach patients. Molecular profiling can help identify the right patients for early studies, and evidence from these trials can then shape broader programs. Over time, this approach could reduce trial-and-error prescribing and bring us closer to personalized care that reflects the biology driving each person’s disease. This is the direction AbbVie believes is essential for translating scientific insight into sustained patient benefit. Looking Ahead: The Next Frontier in Immunology One of the most promising prospects about precision medicine in immunology is the ability to use molecular signatures that transcend traditional disease boundaries, enabling us to recognize patterns that cross several autoimmune conditions. This “plug and play” approach means that, armed with a growing arsenal of treatments, we can match therapies to the specific molecular profile of an individual, regardless of their primary diagnosis. Importantly, we know that molecular profiles can change over time, so the ability to quickly pivot treatments will become a cornerstone of future care. The integration of vast datasets with computational modeling and machine learning enables hypothesis generation and simulation of complex tissue environments using advanced in vitro systems and rank-order potential therapy combinations for specific subgroups. By harnessing these technologies judiciously, we can make smarter, faster decisions about which therapies to advance, achieving better predictions and faster learning cycles without placing additional burden on patients. The prospect of precision medicine in immunology could mean that deep, durable, and even drug-free remission becomes a reality for more patients, allowing individuals to live free from the burden of chronic illness. Much of this work remains in early stages, and not all hypotheses will translate clinically, but this iterative model enables faster learning from both success and failure. This “plug and play” approach means that, armed with a growing arsenal of treatments, we can match therapies to the specific molecular profile of an individual, regardless of their primary diagnosis. Importantly, we know that molecular profiles can change over time, so the ability to quickly pivot treatments will become a cornerstone of future care. The integration of vast datasets with computational modeling and machine learning enables hypothesis generation and simulation of complex tissue environments using advanced in vitro systems and rank-order potential therapy combinations for specific subgroups. By harnessing these technologies judiciously, we can make smarter, faster decisions about which therapies to advance, achieving better predictions and faster learning cycles without placing additional burden on patients. The prospect of precision medicine in immunology could mean that deep, durable, and even drug-free remission becomes a reality for more patients, allowing individuals to live free from the burden of chronic illness. Much of this work remains in early stages, and not all hypotheses will translate clinically, but this iterative model enables faster learning from both success and failure. Kori Wallace is the vice president of Clinical Development for AbbVie Immunology The promise of precision medicine in immunology is both daunting and profound: tailoring treatment to each person’s unique biology to achieve better outcomes. This approach has revolutionized oncology, demonstrating the success of matching treatments to molecular biomarkers. Immunology brings new challenges: immune‑mediated conditions are heterogeneous, influenced by multiple overlapping immune and inflammatory pathways, and can change over time. Yet even in the face of that complexity, new approaches grounded in reverse translation, rational combination strategies, and molecular profiling can pave a path toward higher remission rates and more predictable, patient‑centered care. Current therapies for many immune-mediated diseases often plateau at similar efficacy levels. Even when a new mechanism adds a meaningful bump, a substantial portion of people still do not achieve or maintain response at one year. This “efficacy ceiling” reflects multifactorial biology: suppressing one pathway can leave others active, allowing symptoms to persist or return. For patients, that reality translates into cycling through treatments, preventable flares, and uncertainty. A more precise strategy, built around a person’s molecular drivers, aims to reduce that uncertainty and deliver steadier relief with treatment that reflects the diversity and complexity of each individual’s immune system. At AbbVie, this ambition is shaping how we think about the future of immunology. Reverse Translation: Closing the Gap Between Research and Patient Care Reverse translation is increasingly reshaping immunology research by turning real-world evidence and knowledge generated from ongoing clinical trials of investigational medicines into discovery insights and then cycling those insights back into drug development. Instead of relying solely on preclinical hypotheses, researchers can now systematically analyze clinical data and patient samples to uncover new patterns and mechanisms that might otherwise remain hidden. This approach acknowledges the multifactorial and evolving nature of immune-mediated diseases, where a single pathway rarely tells the whole story. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Immunology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe However, the process is not without hurdles. Obtaining tissue samples, for example, can be invasive and logistically challenging, limiting the granularity of findings. In addition, the heterogeneity intrinsic to autoimmune diseases means that relevant signals are often subtle and complex, not easily captured by a single biomarker. As a result, reverse translation increasingly relies on advanced analytical approaches, using large datasets and sophisticated modeling techniques, to uncover patterns. Despite these challenges, the approach is paving the path toward more precise care in immunology, ensuring that discoveries are continually informed and refined by real-world outcomes. Rational Combination Strategies: Breaking the Efficacy Ceiling The insights gained from reverse translation are now fueling a new era of therapeutic innovation in immunology, the design of rational combination strategies that pair complementary mechanisms to overcome efficacy ceilings. The goal is not simply to “stack” drugs, but to identify pathway synergies that produce biological effects neither agent achieves alone. It is the lessons learned from nonresponders that are proving particularly valuable. By examining what continues to drive disease when symptoms persist, researchers can uncover which immune pathways remain active or insufficiently suppressed. That knowledge informs the design of rational combination approaches that maintain control of the original pathway while adding a second or even a third mechanism to address residual drivers of disease. In immunology, this concept is still maturing, but early data suggest that such combinations can sometimes uncover previously unrecognized biological interactions. Yet even the most promising combinations must ultimately prove their value where it matters most—for real patients in the clinic. The emergence of novel biology is only meaningful if it translates into tangible improvements in remission rates, durability of response, or quality of life. Achieving this requires not just new therapies, but also new ways of identifying which patients are most likely to benefit, which is a challenge that brings molecular profiling to the forefront of precision immunology. Molecular Profiling: Designing Studies That Impact the Biology Driving Disease Molecular profiling is shifting immunology beyond broad diagnostic categories toward a more nuanced, biology-driven approach. By analyzing molecular signatures—unique patterns of genes, proteins, and other biomarkers—researchers can stratify people into subgroups that are more likely to respond to specific therapies. This approach can make it possible to match the right drug, or combination of drugs, to the right person at the right time. In clinical trials, molecular profiling can enable researchers to test insights gained from reverse translation, focusing on whether a targeted pathway is mechanistically relevant to a specific subgroup. These mechanistic trials are key to validating such hypotheses, but they are challenging. Unlike oncology, where tumor shrinkage offers a clear signal, immunology endpoints often involve subjective measures, such as joint tenderness or abdominal pain, and variable biomarkers, making efficacy harder to detect in small cohorts. Regulatory frameworks add complexity, as current standards still favor large, all-comer studies. Adapting regulations to support mechanistic trials will be critical for accelerating innovation. When mechanistic trials confirm a pathway’s relevance, they feed directly into larger studies designed to establish safety and efficacy—steps required before new therapies can reach patients. Molecular profiling can help identify the right patients for early studies, and evidence from these trials can then shape broader programs. Over time, this approach could reduce trial-and-error prescribing and bring us closer to personalized care that reflects the biology driving each person’s disease. This is the direction AbbVie believes is essential for translating scientific insight into sustained patient benefit. The integration of vast datasets with computational modeling and machine learning enables hypothesis generation and simulation of complex tissue environments using advanced in vitro systems and rank-order potential therapy combinations for specific subgroups. By harnessing these technologies judiciously, we can make smarter, faster decisions about which therapies to advance, achieving better predictions and faster learning cycles without placing additional burden on patients. The prospect of precision medicine in immunology could mean that deep, durable, and even drug-free remission becomes a reality for more patients, allowing individuals to live free from the burden of chronic illness. Much of this work remains in early stages, and not all hypotheses will translate clinically, but this iterative model enables faster learning from both success and failure.
